title: practical english usage - Part 190
content:
For the expression If I were you .... ►    240.

If my nose were a little shorter, I'd be quite pretty.

If I were rich, I would spend all my time travelling.

The  grammatical name for this use of were is ‘ subjunctive’  (►  232).

and many people consider it more correct, especially in American English.

and informal styles. In a formal style were is more common than was, We   often use were instead of was after if. This is common in both formal 4 if  I were,      etc For should in the //clause, ►    244.1.

For / should... meaning ‘ I advise you to             240.2.   For would in the //-clause, ►    245.

We’d get up earlier if there was a good reason to.

We   use 'd as a contraction (►  337).

If I knew her name, I should tell you. (rare) American English.

This is now unusual in British English, and has almost disappeared from After I and we, should is sometimes used with the same meaning as would.

3   would,       should        and   'd Would it be all right if I came round about seven tomorrow?

It would be nice if you helped me a bit with the housework.

This structure can make suggestions sound less definite, and so more polite.

grammar *241  unreal past situations If only your father were here!

We   can use were instead of was (►   239.4).

If only I knew more people!  If only I was better-looking!

a  past to talk about the present often stands alone, without a main clause. Tense use is as follows: the same as I wish .. . (►  632), but is more emphatic. The  clause with if only We   can use If only...! to say that we  would like things to be different. It means 242if  only or been here now. (   ... I wouldn't be here now.) If my mother hadn't met my father at a party thirty years ago, I wouldn't have or can do it. (   It would be nice. ..) It would have been nice to go to Australia this winter, but there's no way we turned out.

future situations which  are no longer possible because of the way things have We   sometimes use structures with would have. . .to talk about present and 3 present      use:  situations       that    are   no   longer     possible If I hadn't been so tired, I might have realised what was happening.

If he'd run a bit faster, he could have won.

possibly have .. /.

and might have + past participle to mean 'would perhaps have ...' or ‘ would We   can use could have + past participle to mean 'would have been able to ..

2  could      have     .. . and   might       have      ...

I'd have been in bad trouble if Megan hadn't helped me.

If you had worked harder, you would have passed your exam.

oh                                                  or asked ww...  If y&u-asked me.. .  ... / had toklyou;) not If you had asked me, I would have told you. (     If you would have the //-clause, and would have + past participle in the other part of the sentence.

To  talk about past situations that did not happen, we  use a past perfect tense in 1  if  + past    perfect;     would       have      + past    participle If you had worked harder, you would have passed your exam.

241 unreal     past    situations In this case, I should/would is similar to you should.

I shouldn't worry. I would get that car serviced.

or I would... to give advice.

Sometimes we  leave out If I were you, and just use I should... (British English) 2 I should/would             ...

If I was you is also possible. Some people consider it incorrect (►  239.4).

If I were you, I'd get that car serviced.

grammar •  243   if.. . will If you would come this way...                      -> Would can be used to make a request even more polite.

If your mother will complete this form, I'll prepare her ticket.

If you will come this way, I'll show you your room.

it means 'are willing to’  (►   80.1).

We   can use if + will in polite requests. In this case, will is not a future auxiliary; 4 polite     requests:      If  you     will    come      this    way    ...

I don't know if I'll be ready in time, (     .. .if I'm ready in time.) We   can use will after z/in indirect questions (►  260.3).

3  indirect      questions:      I don't     know      if  .. .
